name: Quercetin Dihydrate
historicalUse: Quercetin-rich plants — onion, elder, buckwheat — have been used across Greek, Roman, Arabic, and Chinese medicinal traditions for millennia. Ibn Sina described onion preparations for respiratory inflammation and immune support. The flavonoid was isolated and characterized in the 20th century but its botanical sources appear in virtually every classical pharmacopoeia.
modernEvidence: Quercetin is the most abundant dietary flavonoid and one of the most studied. Mechanisms include mast cell stabilization (anti-allergic), NLRP3 inflammasome inhibition, Nrf2 activation, and senolytic activity (elimination of senescent cells). Clinical trials show efficacy in allergic rhinitis, exercise-induced inflammation, and as a senolytic agent in combination with dasatinib.
mechanisms: Mast cell membrane stabilization — inhibits histamine and leukotriene release
NLRP3 inflammasome inhibition — reduces IL-1β and IL-18 maturation
Senolytic activity — selectively induces apoptosis in senescent cells
Zinc ionophore — facilitates intracellular zinc transport, supporting antiviral defense
Nrf2 pathway activation — upregulates HO-1 and glutathione synthesis
benefits: Contributes to cellular resilience and healthy aging
safetyProfile: Well tolerated. GRAS status. Some reports of headache at very high doses (>1g/day). Dihydrate form improves stability and dissolution.
contraindications: Caution in estrogen-sensitive conditions — quercetin has mild estrogenic activity in some studies
High doses not recommended during pregnancy (lack of safety data)
drugInteractions: Cyclosporine — quercetin inhibits CYP3A4; may increase cyclosporine levels
Fluoroquinolone antibiotics — quercetin may reduce absorption if taken simultaneously; separate by 2 hours
Tamoxifen — potential interaction via CYP2C8 modulation
physicianNotes: Particularly useful in patients with allergic or inflammatory conditions. Senolytic properties are of interest in longevity medicine. Exercise caution in transplant patients on cyclosporine. Dihydrate form preferred for bioavailability over aglycone.
patientGuidance: Take with meals. If you take immunosuppressant medications or blood thinners, inform your physician. Separate from antibiotic doses by at least 2 hours.
